sections.label: Section front.cast
sections.original:
FABULAE INTERLOCUTORES .

ANTIPHO , adolescens , filius Demiphonis . CHREMIES , senex , frater Demiphonis . CRATINUS , advocati . CRITO , advocati . HEGIO , advocati . DAVUS , servus . DEMIPHO , senex , frater Chremetis . DORIO , leno . GETA , servus Demiphonis . NAUSISTRATA , matrona , uxor Chremetis . PHAEDRIA , adolescens , filius Chremetis . PHORMIO , parasitus . SOPHRONA , nutrix Phanii .
sections.translation: Dramatis Personae DEMIPHO , [*] Aged Athenians , brothers . CHREMES , [*] ANTIPHO , [*] son of Demipho . PHAEDRIA , [*] son of Chremes . PHORMIO , [*] a Parasite . GETA , [*] servant of Demipho . DAVUS , [*] a servant . HEGIO , Advocates . [*] CRATINUS , [*] CRITO , [*] DORIO , [*] a Procurer . NAUSISTRATA , [*] the wife of Chremes . SOPHRONA , [*] the nurse of Phanium .

sections.label: Section front.production
sections.original: PHORMIO . ACTA LUDIS ROMANIS , L . POSTUMIO ALBINO , L . CORNELIO MERULA , AEDILIBUS CURULIBUS . EGERE L . AMBIVIUS TURPIO , L . ATILIUS PRAENEST1NUS . MODOS FECIT FLACCUS CLAUDII , TIBIIS IMPARIBUS . TOTA GRAECA APOLLODORU EPIDICAZOMENOS . FACTA EST IV . C . FANNIO , M . VALERIO , COSS .
sections.translation:
THE TITLE OF THE PLAY .
PERFORMED at the Roman Games , [*] L . Posthumius Albinus and L . Cornelius Merula being Curule Aediles . L . Ambivius Turpio and L . Atilius Praenestinus performed it . Flaccus , the freedman of Claudius , composed the music to a base and a treble flute . It is wholly from the Greek , being the Epidicazomenos of Apollodorus . It was represented four times , [*] C . Fannius and M . Valerius being Consuls . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.1
sections.original: POSTQUAM poeta vetus poetam non potest
sections.translation: SINCE the old Poet [*] can not withdraw our bard from his pursuits and reduce him to indolence , he endeavors , by invectives , to deter him from writing : for he is wont to say to this effect ,— that the Plays which he has hitherto composed

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.5
sections.original: Tenui esse oratione et scriptura levi ;
sections.translation: are poor in their language , and of meagre style ; because he has nowhere described a frantic youth as seeing a hind in flight , and the hounds pursuing ; while he implores [*] and entreated that he would give her aid . But if he had been aware that his Play , when formerly first represented ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.10
sections.original: Actoris opera magis stetisse quam sua ,
sections.translation: stood its ground more through the merits of the performers than its own , he would attack with much less boldness than he does . Now , if there is any one who says or thinks to this effect , that if the old Poet had not assailed him first , the young one could have devised no Prologue

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.15
sections.original: Quem diceret , nisi haberet cui malediceret ;
sections.translation: for him to repeat , without having some one to abuse , let him receive this for an answer : that the prize is proposed in common to all who apply to the Dramatic art . He has aimed at driving our Poet from his studies to absolute want ; he then has in-tended this for an answer , not an attack .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.20
sections.original: Benedictis si certasset , audisset bene .
sections.translation: If he had opposed him with fair words , he would have heard himself civilly addressed ; what has been given by him , let him consider as now returned . I will make an end of speaking about him , when , of his own accord , he himself makes an end of offending .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.25
sections.original: Epidicazomenon qnam vocant comoediam
sections.translation: Now give your attention to what I request . I present you a new Play , which they call Epidicazomenos , [*] in Greek : in the Latin , he calls it Phormio ; because the person that acts the principal part is Phormio , a Parasite , through whom , principally , the plot will be ’ carried on , if your favor attends the Poet .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.30
sections.original: Date operam : adeste aequo animo per silentiumr ;
sections.translation: Lend your attention ; in silence give an ear with impartial feelings , that we may not experience a like fortune to what we did , when , through a tumult , our Company was driven from the place ; [*] which place , the merit of the actor , and your good-will and candor seconding it , has since restored unto us .

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: Amicus summus meus et popularis Geta
sections.translation: Geta , my very good friend and fellow-townsman , came to me yesterday . There had been for some time a trifling balance of money of his in my hands upon a small account ; he asked me to make it up . I have done so , and am carrying it to him . But I hear that his master ’ s son has taken

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: Uxorem : ei , credo , munus hoc corraditur .
sections.translation: a wife ; this , I suppose , is scraped together as a present for her . How unfair a custom !— that those who have the least should always be giving something to the more wealthy ! That which the poor wretch has with difficulty spared , ounce by ounce , out of his allowance , [*] defrauding himself of every indulgence ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.original: Id illa universum abripiet , haud existimans
sections.translation: the whole of it will she carry of , without thinking with how much labor it has been acquired . And then besides , Geta will be struck [*] for another present [*] when his mistress is brought to bed ; and then again for another present , when the child ’ s birthday comes ; when they initiate him , [*] too : all this the mother will carry off ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: Puer causa erit mittendi . Sed videon Getam ?
sections.translation: the child will only be the pretext for the present . But don ’ t I see Geta there ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.51
sections.original: Si quis me quaeret rufus —
sections.translation: If any red-haired man should inquire for me —

sections.label: Section 1.2.51b
sections.original: Praesto est ; desine .
sections.translation: Here he is , say no more .

sections.label: Section 1.2.51c
sections.original: Oh !
sections.translation: Oh ! Why I was trying to come and meet you , Davus .

sections.label: Section 1.2.52b
sections.original: Accipe , hem ,
sections.translation: Here , take it ; it ’ s all ready counted out ; [*] the number just amounts to the sum I owed you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.54
sections.original: Amo te , et non neglexisse habeo gratiam .
sections.translation: I am obliged to you ; and I return you thanks for not having forgotten me .

sections.label: Section 1.2.55
sections.original: Praesertim ut nunc sunt mores . Adeo res redit ;
sections.translation: Especially as people ’ s ways are nowadays ; things are come to such a pass , if a person repays you any thing , you must be greatly obliged to him . But why are you out of spirits ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.57b
sections.original: Egone ? neseis quo in metu et
sections.translation: What , I ? You little know what terror and peril I am in .

sections.label: Section 1.2.58b
sections.original: Quid istuc est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.58c
sections.original: Scies ,
sections.translation: You shall know , if you can only keep it secret .

sections.label: Section 1.2.59b
sections.original: Abi sis , insciens .
sections.translation: Out upon you , simpleton ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.60
sections.original: Cuius tu fidem in pecunia perspexeris ,
sections.translation: the man , whose trustworthiness you have experienced as to money , are you afraid to intrust with words ? In what way have I any interest in deceiving you ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.62b
sections.original: Ergo ausculta .
sections.translation: Well then , listen .

sections.label: Section 1.2.62c
sections.original: Hanc operam tibi dico .
sections.translation: I give you my best attention .

sections.label: Section 1.2.63
sections.original: Senis nostri , Dave , fratrem maiorem Chremem
sections.translation: Davus , do you know Chremes , the elder brother of our old gentleman ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.64b
sections.original: Quidni ?
sections.translation: Why should I not ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.64c
sections.original: Quid ? eius gnatum Phaedriam ?
sections.translation: Well , and his son Phaedria ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.65
sections.original: Tamquam te .
sections.translation: As well as your own self .

sections.label: Section 1.2.65b
sections.original: Evenit senibus ainbobus simul ,
sections.translation: It so happened to both the old gentlemen , just at the same period , that the one had to take a journey to Lemnos , and our old man to Cilicia , to see an old acquaintance ; he tempted over the old man by letters , promising him all but mountains of gold .

sections.label: Section 1.2.69
sections.original: Cui tanta erat res , et supererat ?
sections.translation: To one who had so much property , that he had more than he could use ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.69b
sections.original: Desinas :
sections.translation: Do have done ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.70
sections.original: Sic est ingeniun .
sections.translation: that is his way .

sections.label: Section 1.2.70b
sections.original: Oh , regem me esse oportuit .
sections.translation: Oh , as for that , I really ought to have been a man of fortune .

sections.label: Section 1.2.71
sections.original: Abeuntes ambo hic tum senes , me filiis
sections.translation: When departing hence , both the old gentlemen left ne as a sort of tutor to their sons .

sections.label: Section 1.2.72b
sections.original: O Geta , provinciam
sections.translation: Ah , Geta , you undertook a hard task there .

sections.label: Section 1.2.73b
sections.original: Mihi usus venit , hoc scio :
sections.translation: I came to experience it , I know that . I ’ m quite sure that I was forsaken by my good Genius , who must have been angry with me . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.2.75
sections.original: Coepi adversari primo . Quid verbis opus est ?
sections.translation: I began to oppose them at first ; but what need of talking ? As long as I was trusty to the old men , I was paid for it in my shoulder-blades .

sections.label: Section 1.2.77
sections.original: Venere in mentem mili istaec ; Namque inscitia est ,
sections.translation: This , then , occurred to my mind : why , this is folly to kick against the spur . [*] I began to do every thing for them that they wished to be humored in .

sections.label: Section 1.2.79b
sections.original: Scisti uti foro .
sections.translation: You knew how to make your market . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.2.80
sections.original: Noster mali nihil quicquam primo . Hic Phaedria
sections.translation: Our young fellow did no mischief whatever at first ; that Phaedria at once picked up a certain damsel , a Music-girl , and fell in love with her to distraction . She belonged to a most abominable Procurer ; and their fathers had taken good care that they should have nothing to give him .

sections.label: Section 1.2.85
sections.original: Restabat aliud nihil nisi oculos pascere ,
sections.translation: There remained nothing for him then but to feed his eyes , to follow her about , to escort her to the school , [*] and to escort her back again . We , having nothing to do , lent our aid to Phaedria . Near the school at which she was taught , right opposite the place , there was a certain barber ’ s shop : here we were generally in the habit

sections.label: Section 1.2.90
sections.original: Plerumque eam opperiri dum inde iret domum .
sections.translation: of waiting for her , until she was coming home again . In the mean time , while one day we were sitting there , there came in a young man in tears ; [*] we were surprised at this . We inquired what was the matter ? Never , said he , has poverty appeared to me a burden so grievous and so insupportable as just now .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: Modo quandam vidi virginem hic viciniae
sections.translation: I have just seen a certain poor young woman in this neighborhood lamenting her dead mother . She was laid out before her , and not a single friend , acquaintance , or relation was there with her , except one poor old woman , to assist her in the funeral : I pitied her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: Virgo ipsa facie egregia . Quid verbis opus est ?
sections.translation: The girl herself was of surpassing beauty . What need of a long story ? She moved us all . At once Antipho exclaims , Would you like us to go and visit her ? The other said , I think we ought — let us go — show us the way , please . We went , and arrived there ; we saw her ; the girl was beautiful , and that you might say so the more ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: Nihil aderat adiumenti ad pulchritudinem .
sections.translation: there was no heightening to her beauty ; her hair disheveled , her feet bare , herself neglected , and in tears ; her dress : mean , so ’ that , had there not been an excess of beauty in her very charms , these circumstances must have extinguished those charms . The one who had lately fallen in love with the Music-girl

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: Satis , inquit , scita est : noster vero —
sections.translation: said : She is well enough ; but our youth —

sections.label: Section 1.2.110b
sections.original: Iam scio :
sections.translation: I know it already — fell in love with her .

sections.label: Section 1.2.111b
sections.original: Scin quam ? Quo evadat vide .
sections.translation: Can you imagine to what an extent ? Observe the consequence . The day after , he goes straight to the old woman ; entreats her to let him have her : she , on the other hand , refuses him , and says that he is not acting properly ; that , she is a citizen of Athens ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: Bonam , bonis prognatam : si uxorem velit ,
sections.translation: virtuous , and born of honest parents : that if he wishes to make her his wife , he is at liberty to do so according to law ; but if otherwise , she gives him a refusal . Our youth was at a loss what to . do . He was both eager to marry her , and he dreaded his absent father .

sections.label: Section 1.2.119
sections.original: Non , si redisset , ei pater veniam daret ?
sections.translation: Would not his father , if he had returned , have given him leave ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: Ille indotatam virginem atque ignobilem
sections.translation: He let him marry a girl with no fortune , and of obscure birth ! He would never do so .

sections.label: Section 1.2.121b
sections.original: Quid fit denique ?
sections.translation: What came of it at last ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.122
sections.original: Quid fiat ? Est parasitus quidam Phormio ,
sections.translation: What came of it ? There is one Phormio here , a Parasite , a fellow of great assurance ; may all the Gods confound him !

sections.label: Section 1.2.124
sections.original: Quid is fecit ?
sections.translation: What has he done ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.124b
sections.original: Hoc consilium quod dicam dedit .
sections.translation: He has given this piece of advice , which I will tell you of .

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: Lex est , ut orbae qui sunt genere proximi
sections.translation: There is a law , that orphan girls are to marry those who are their next-of-kin ; and the same law commands such persons to marry them . I ’ ll say you are the next-of-kin , and take out a summons [*] against you ; I ’ ll pretend that I am a friend of the girl ’ s father ; we will come before the judges : who her father was ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.130
sections.original: Quae mater , qui cognata tibi sit , omnia haec
sections.translation: who her mother , how she is related to you — all this I ’ ll trump up , just as will be advantageous and suited to my purpose ; on your disproving none of these things , I shall prevail , of course . Your father will return ; a quarrel will be the consequence ; what care I ? She will still be ours .

sections.label: Section 1.2.134b
sections.original: Iocularem audaciam !
sections.translation: An amusing piece of assurance !
